brief_document.title: Child workflows: ChildHandle, spawn, await
brief_document.purpose: Let a workflow spawn other workflows as typed children. workflow.spawn yields a ChildHandle(output, error); spawn_and_wait blocks for the result; child.await collects a handle's recorded result. The child's output and error types are carried through.
brief_document.task: Implement aion/child: ChildHandle(o, e); workflow.spawn(name, workflow_fn, input, codecs) -> ChildHandle(o, e) and workflow.spawn_and_wait(...) -> Result(o, e) wrappers; child.await(ChildHandle(o, e)) -> Result(o, e). Bind to AT's child-workflow spawner via the ffi layer, encoding the input and decoding the recorded child result.

requirements.title: ChildHandle and spawn
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define ChildHandle(o, e) and workflow.spawn(name, workflow_fn, input, codecs) -> ChildHandle(o, e) starting a child workflow as a linked execution, carrying the child output and error types.
acceptance: child.gleam defines an opaque ChildHandle(o, e) carrying the child's correlation/handle and the output/error Codecs.

workflow.spawn(name, workflow_fn: fn(i) -> Result(o, e), input: i, codecs) encodes the input and binds to AT's child-spawn mechanism (linked process, ChildWorkflowStarted recorded), returning a ChildHandle(o, e).
The workflow_fn's o and e types flow into the ChildHandle so await/spawn_and_wait return Result(o, e).
A harness test spawns a typed child and asserts the handle carries the expected types (compiles) and a started observation is captured.

requirements.title: await and spawn_and_wait
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define child.await(ChildHandle(o, e)) -> Result(o, e) collecting the recorded child result, and workflow.spawn_and_wait(...) -> Result(o, e) as the spawn-then-await convenience.
acceptance: child.await(ChildHandle(o, e)) blocks for the child result via AT's await mechanism, decoding the recorded ChildWorkflowCompleted result with the output Codec or returning the decoded child error on ChildWorkflowFailed.
workflow.spawn_and_wait(name, workflow_fn, input, codecs) -> Result(o, e) is spawn followed by await.
On replay the recorded child result is returned without re-spawning (AT D8/AD); the SDK relies on AT/AD via ffi.
A harness test (mocking the child result) asserts spawn_and_wait returns the decoded typed Ok and that a failing child returns the decoded typed Err.

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement child-workflow lifecycle, linking, supervision, or result recording — those are AT-008 and AE. This brief binds to them.
SHALL NOT implement all/race/map — that is AF-010.
SHALL NOT let child input/output cross the boundary without Codecs (CO9).
verification: gleam test passes for spawn, await, and spawn_and_wait tests under the harness.
No panic/todo; child types are carried through the handle.

checklist.text: workflow.spawn(name, workflow_fn, input, codecs) -> ChildHandle(o, e) and workflow.spawn_and_wait(...) -> Result(o, e) carry the child output and error types.
child.await(ChildHandle(o, e)) -> Result(o, e) collects the recorded child result.

stories.text: As a workflow author, I want to spawn child workflows and fan activities out with all/race/map, with the result types preserved, so that concurrency stays type-safe.

constraints.text: Pure Gleam package. No Rust, no engine code, no beamr dependency inside aion_flow. It publishes to Hex standalone (gleam_stdlib is its only runtime dependency, plus gleam_json for the default JSON codecs).
Every @external(erlang, ...) declaration lives in the single internal module aion/internal/ffi. No author-facing module declares an @external (per D1).

Module files contain logic; the package root and any mod-equivalent re-export aggregator stay declarations/re-exports only. No god files — split before a module exceeds ~500 lines of code (excluding comments/whitespace).

No partial implementations. Every public function is complete and total: no panic, no todo, no assert-as-control-flow; failure paths return typed Result errors. Gleam has no exceptions for control flow — every fallible binding returns Result.

Type safety is enforced, not advisory: activity input/output, signal payload, query return, and child input/output types are static type parameters; an invalid composition fails `gleam build`. A type-safety test brief asserts representative invalid compositions are rejected (compile-fail expectations documented).

Determinism: the SDK exposes workflow.now/workflow.random bound to AD's determinism context and exposes NO binding to a wall clock or entropy source. Workflow-code documentation states the prohibition explicitly.

The recorded/side-effectful boundary is structural: the only side-effect dispatch the SDK offers is workflow.run(activity) (and all/race/map over activities). There is no generic side_effect(fn) escape hatch (per D4).

No hardcoded defaults for configurable values: no default RetryPolicy, no default timeout, no default backoff numbers baked into the SDK. An activity without a retry decorator runs once; the author supplies all policy values (CLAUDE.md no-assumed-defaults).
User data crosses the boundary only through a Codec(a); a decode failure is a typed DecodeError, never a panic. No primitive silently swallows a decode error.

aion_flow does not implement engine mechanics (timer firing, signal routing, replay) — those are AE/AD/AT, reached via @external. It does not author NIFs in Rust — that is AN. It is not packaged into .aion — that is AP. The cluster owns only the Gleam API surface and its binding declarations.
All acceptance uses Gleam tooling: gleam build, gleam check, gleam format --check, gleam test must all pass clean. The test harness (aion/testing) runs under gleam test with no external services.

resolved_context.intention: Define aion_flow, the Gleam package (published to Hex) that workflow authors import to write durable workflows. It is the human-facing surface of the entire engine: a workflow author writes a plain, statically-typed Gleam function — decisions, loops, data transformation — and reaches the outside world only through this SDK's activity, signal, query, timer, and child-workflow primitives. The package is pure Gleam with no engine code inside it; every primitive is a thin @external(erlang) binding that resolves at runtime, when beamr loads the compiled workflow and the engine has registered the matching NIFs (AE-004), to the engine-side mechanics in AE/AD/AT. The defining property is compile-time type safety: activity inputs and outputs, signal payloads, and query returns are all statically typed, so an invalid composition — passing a String where an activity expects an Order, awaiting a signal of the wrong type, returning the wrong type from a query handler — fails at `gleam build`, not three days into a long-running production workflow. This is the concrete advantage over Temporal's SDKs (Go weakly typed, TypeScript better but runtime-checked). The package also ships aion/testing, the Temporal-equivalent test harness: simulate time so a month-long sleep resolves instantly, mock activities to return canned results, and assert that a workflow replays to the same state — all in plain gleam test, with no engine, no beamr, and no store.
